cataloging_subject.label: The Wintney Version of the Rule of St Benedict
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The anonymous English prose adaptation and revision of Æthelwold's Old English Rule of St Benedict made for female religious readers and preserved uniquely in British Library Cotton MS Claudius D III. Its intellectual structure is one prologue followed by chapters I-LXXIII. The cataloged work is not the alternating Latin text in the manuscript, the whole trilingual codex, Arnold Schröer's 1888 bilingual edition, the 1978 reprint and Mechthild Gretsch supplement, the University of Michigan image carrier or digital text, or the checked-in XML artifact.

catalog_summary.title: The Wintney Version of the Rule of St Benedict
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous reviser or revisers of Æthelwold's Old English Rule
catalog_summary.date: Revised by the first quarter of the thirteenth century; first attested in the early-thirteenth-century Cotton Claudius D III witness
catalog_summary.region: England; associated with Wintney Priory, Hampshire, but exact revision and copying places are unknown

identifiers.scheme: University of Michigan DLPS source item

titles.value: The Wintney Version of the Rule of St Benedict

assertion.notes: A normalized discovery title using the British Library's current spelling Wintney.

titles.value: The Winteney Version of the Rule of St Benedict
notes: Winteney is the conventional spelling in Schröer's edition and much subsequent scholarship.

titles.value: Her onȝynð ures halies fæder reȝolan sanctes Benedictes

assertion.notes: The spelling follows the encoded Schröer text; EM1060's direct witness transcription differs slightly.

titles.value: Ece lif to leane and wunung mid Gode ealle þam þe þisse reȝole blydelice fyliad

agents.name: Anonymous Wintney adapter and reviser or revisers
notes: A discovery label that preserves Artamonova's warning that the surviving text may reflect more than one stage of revision; it does not assert a known group or one identifiable person.

agents.name: Arnold Schröer
notes: Editor of the 1888 bilingual source edition; not the medieval author.

assertion.notes: The adaptation refashions Æthelwold's vernacular Rule for female religious use; no adapter is named.
Artamonova allows multiple revision layers; this record therefore does not collapse the history into one securely dated act by one person.

scope.description: The target English work as presented in the 1888 source edition

notes: This is a transmission-layer responsibility and does not make Benedict or Schröer the author of the Wintney adaptation.

date_statements.display: By the first quarter of the thirteenth century; possibly through more than one earlier revision stage

assertion.notes: The surviving early-thirteenth-century copy supplies a terminus ante quem, not proof that every adaptation stage occurred when or where that copy was made.

date_statements.display: Early thirteenth century, probably soon after 1200

assertion.notes: This dates the unique witness and first known attestation, not a securely isolated original adaptation event.

places.label: Wintney Priory, Hampshire
notes: A historical religious-house site used here for association and provenance, not a confirmed composition or copying place.

assertion.notes: The English vernacular tradition and English witness support a broad cultural origin only.

scope.description: Historical use, ownership, and adaptation-for-nuns context

assertion.notes: The current British Library catalog confirms Wintney ownership from the thirteenth to fifteenth centuries, while EM1060 gives the manuscript's origin as unknown. Association must not be promoted to a proven place of adaptation or copying.

language_statements.label: Middle English

assertion.notes: Middle English is retained as the single discovery facet because the U-M corpus catalogs the text that way and the copy is early thirteenth century. The British Library catalogs the same language as Old English; Artamonova explains that the text remains essentially Æthelwold's Old English translation while its spelling, inflection, and some passages were updated. Adding a second ang facet would misleadingly make one transitional English text appear bilingual.

notes: This periodization choice does not erase the text's Old English linguistic and textual base. The alternating Latin chapters belong to a separate work and are outside this work-language facet.

form_statements.description: One continuous prose rule organized as a prologue and seventy-three chapters; the repository target DIV1 contains no L or LG verse elements.

assertion.notes: The work adapts an earlier vernacular translation rather than freshly translating the exact facing Latin text in Cotton Claudius D III.

subject_statements.label: Admission, profession, visitation, discipline, and community governance
notes: The adaptation regularly feminizes offices and community members as abbess, nuns, and sisters.

subject_statements.label: Divine Office, choir, Mass, communion, refectory rites, and the liturgical year

summaries.value: An anonymous English adaptation of Æthelwold's Old English Rule of St Benedict for a female religious community. A prologue frames obedience and conversion as the way to eternal life; seventy-three chapters regulate kinds of religious, the abbess, counsel, humility, worship, discipline, property, food and drink, work and reading, hospitality, admission, offices, travel, mutual obedience, and communal charity. The work remains textually and syntactically close to its Old English base while updating forms and rewriting some passages.

The U-M encoding includes the 1888 title page and a German section heading, keys only the English prologue and chapters from the bilingual edition, and retains page-break placeholders for omitted front matter, Latin pages, and back matter. Those carrier and editorial layers are not parts of the medieval work.

The Old English versus Middle English label is a real scholarly periodization problem, not evidence of two languages in the target work. This record uses Middle English for discovery while preserving the British Library's Old English label and Artamonova's account of an early-thirteenth-century revision that remains essentially Æthelwold's Old English translation.

extent.description: One prose adaptation with one prologue and seventy-three chapters. The target Middle English DIV1 in the repository XML contains 75 DIV2 containers because chapter VII is split into N='7' and N='7b'; that encoding split does not create a seventy-fourth chapter. The same DIV1 contains 187 P, 74 PB, 231 MILESTONE, 504 NOTE1, 15 DIV3, and no L or LG elements. Its 74 PB include an omitted-front-matter placeholder and a German edition heading in addition to 72 printed English text pages; the complete file's 206 PB serialize the edition's non-linear image order and are not 206 target pages.

notes: This retained edition layer contributes no medieval work item and does not make German a work-level language.

content_parts.label: The Wintney Version of the Rule of St Benedict
content_parts.summary: The complete surviving English adaptation: prologue plus chapters I-LXXIII.

locators.value: prologue and chapters I-LXXIII; chapter VII split into XML navigation units 7 and 7b
English prologue on pp. 3, 5, 7, 9; chapters I-LXXIII on odd pp. 13-147
English prologue on canvases 35, 37, 39, 41; chapters on odd canvases 45-179; drawn f. 54r facsimile plate on canvas 209
English passages from f. 54r through f. 140r, interleaved with Latin within the bilingual Rule
target begins canvas 117 (f. 54r) and ends canvas 289 (f. 140r); intervening canvases also include alternating Latin

notes: The parent carries the one unique work count; its prologue and chapter-series children carry zero additional work items.
The current manuscript foliation is two leaves ahead of Schröer's old a/b folio milestones: XML 52a corresponds to current f. 54r, and XML 138a occurs on current f. 140r.

content_parts.label: Prologue
content_parts.summary: Calls the reader to obedient listening, conversion, spiritual warfare, and progress toward eternal life.

content_parts.label: Chapters I-LXXIII
content_parts.summary: Seventy-three chapters governing doctrine, offices, worship, discipline, material life, labor, hospitality, formation, hierarchy, and mutual charity in a female religious community.
notes: The XML's separate DIV2 N='7b' continues chapter VII's twelve degrees of humility and is an encoding/editorial split, not an independently numbered medieval chapter.

related_works.title: Æthelwold's Old English translation of the Rule of St Benedict
notes: The relationship is work-to-work. No specific surviving Old English manuscript is asserted as the physical exemplar of the Wintney reviser or Cotton copyist.

related_works.title: Rule of St Benedict
notes: The Latin Rule is the ultimate source work. Artamonova reports that the reviser and the compiler of Cotton Claudius D III used different Latin texts, so the exact facing Latin witness is not treated as the English adaptation's direct exemplar.

evidence.locator: HEADER; FRONT/TITLEPAGE; BODY/DIV1[@TYPE='Middle English version']; omitted Latin and paratext DIV1 placeholders
evidence.summary: Pins item AGV8488.0001.001 and Schröer's 1888 source, states that the text is uncorrected OCR linked to 206 page images, preserves the German title page and heading, and encodes the English prologue and chapters I-LXXIII while serializing omitted Latin and edition matter as page-break placeholders.
notes: Pinned CME commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c; git blob 57fb4998bf0d321c8e5ad12804345f1e24927483; 220,469 bytes.
The header's sole ger language declaration describes the edition-level catalog record and is not reliable work-level evidence for the keyed English text.

evidence.citation: XPath, attribute, page-topology, and checksum inspection of agv8488.xml performed 2026-07-15.
evidence.summary: The target DIV1 has 75 DIV2, 15 DIV3, 89 HEAD, 187 P, 74 PB, 231 MILESTONE, and 504 NOTE1 elements, with no L or LG. The 75 DIV2 units are one prologue, chapters 1-6, chapter 7 split as 7 and 7b, and chapters 8-73. All 206 file-wide PB REF and SEQ values are unique; page references place English on TIFFs 31, 33, 35, 37, then 41 through 175 odd, with title and editorial heading layers separately retained.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse, book-by-book coding instructions for batch BY, item AGV8488.
evidence.locator: En-face, Key and code the following pages, Structure, Notes, and Milestones
evidence.summary: The item-specific instructions identify the facing Latin-even/English-odd layout, direct keying of English only, title page plus odd printed pages [1]-9 and 13-147, a prologue followed by chapters I-LXXIII, prose treatment, and retained folio and edition-reference milestones.
notes: The prose wording says printed page [1] is in scope, while the listed keyed TIFF range begins at 31 (printed page 3); the XML separately retains the page [1] German heading at TIFF 29.
The exact instructions page was retrieved and inspected during research; final parallel URL checking intermittently received HTTP 403 from the U-M host for automated requests.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library Digital Collections, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse item AGV8488.0001.001 and collection index.
evidence.locator: Item metadata, public-domain notice, DPLA No Copyright-United States statement, and indexed English text
evidence.summary: The official indexed item identifies Schröer's 1888 edition, categorizes it in the Middle English corpus, exposes the keyed English text, and states that the collection materials are public domain with a No Copyright-United States rights statement.
notes: The exact item and stable resolver returned a Cloudflare 403 challenge to command-line inspection on 2026-07-15; the official search index and collection browse page remained discoverable.

evidence.citation: Arnold Schröer, ed., Die Winteney-Version der Regula S. Benedicti: Lateinisch und Englisch, mit Einleitung, Anmerkungen, Glossar und einem Facsimile zum erstenmale herausgegeben (Halle: Max Niemeyer, 1888), BSB bsb11663190.
evidence.locator: Title page canvas 5; introduction canvases 13-32; English pp. 3, 5, 7, 9 and odd pp. 13-147 on canvases 35, 37, 39, 41 and odd 45-179; manuscript facsimile plate canvas 209
evidence.summary: The inspected complete 216-canvas edition scan establishes Schröer's full title and responsibility, the bilingual alternating-page presentation, the one prologue and seventy-three English chapters, the unique Cotton Claudius D III witness, Schröer's description of the English text as an adaptation of Æthelwold, and the drawn facsimile plate of the English incipit leaf.
notes: The checksum is for the ignored complete local IIIF bundle recorded in the linked lineage manifest, not an assertion that the provider scan may be redistributed.
Archive validation found 216 ordered inventory items and 218 exact members, with clean ZIP CRC and every image byte count and SHA-256 matching inventory.json.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Cotton MS Claudius D III, record 040-001102527.
evidence.locator: Contents, Rule locus, languages, date, physical description, origin, provenance, access, and digitised content
evidence.summary: The current catalog identifies a parchment codex from England, describes the bilingual Latin and Old English Rule at ff. 52r-140r, distinguishes adjacent contents, records Wintney Priory ownership in the thirteenth through fifteenth centuries, and links the complete 349-canvas digitized object.

evidence.citation: Orietta Da Rold et al., London, British Library, Cotton Claudius D. iii, The Production and Use of English Manuscripts 1060-1220 (EM1060).
evidence.locator: Summary; manuscript item; incipits and explicits; foliation; hand description; history
evidence.summary: The scholarly manuscript record dates the relevant hand to the early thirteenth century, says it was probably written soon after 1200, gives the English incipit at f. 54r and explicit at f. 140r, explains the superseded foliation, and distinguishes unknown origin from Wintney provenance.

evidence.citation: Maria Artamonova, Construing Old English in the Thirteenth Century: The Syntax of the Winteney Adaptation of the Benedictine Rule, Leeds Studies in English, New Series 40 (2009), 27-45.
evidence.summary: Artamonova dates the revision to the first quarter of the thirteenth century, explains the Old/Middle English classification problem, identifies the text as essentially Æthelwold's Old English translation with updated forms and substantially rewritten chapters, preserves the possibility of multiple revision layers, and reports that the reviser and Cotton compiler used different Latin texts.
notes: The complete 24-page provider PDF is 199,408 bytes; its access and rights layer is recorded separately in the lineage manifest.

evidence.citation: H. Hargreaves, review of Die Winteney-Version der Regula S. Benedicti, Scriptorium 34.1 (1980), 177-178.
evidence.summary: Hargreaves characterizes the work as an adaptation for English nuns surviving in the unique Cotton Claudius D III witness, notes the Old-versus-Middle English issue, describes the chapter-by-chapter Latin/English alternation, and identifies the 1978 volume as an exact reprint of Schröer's text with a roughly thirteen-page Gretsch supplement.

evidence.citation: Inventory, folio-label, and direct image inspection of the complete British Library Cotton MS Claudius D III IIIF bundle, 2026-07-15.
evidence.summary: The 349-canvas manifest maps canvas 113 to f. 52r, canvas 117 to the directly inspected English incipit at f. 54r, canvas 289 to the directly inspected English explicit at f. 140r, and canvas 290 to the following obituary calendar at f. 140v. English passages remain interleaved with Latin within the c.117-289 range.
notes: The checksum is for the ignored complete local IIIF bundle recorded in the linked lineage manifest.
Archive validation found 349 ordered inventory items and 351 exact members, with clean ZIP CRC and every image byte count and SHA-256 matching inventory.json.

evidence.citation: General Corpus lineage manifest for agv8488.
evidence.summary: Separates the repository artifact, U-M encoding and historical page-image carrier, Schröer edition, Cotton witness, complete BSB edition facsimile, complete British Library manuscript facsimile, abstract source works, 1978 reprint and Gretsch supplement, scholarship, access, caches, and rights statements.

open_questions.question: How many historical revision stages produced the Wintney adaptation, and can any surviving Old English or Latin witness be identified as a direct physical exemplar?
next_steps: Consult Gretsch's 1978 Anglia article and Jayatilaka's full textual study.
Distinguish work-level agreement with Æthelwold's tradition from evidence of a specific physical exemplar.
Do not identify the exact facing Latin text in Cotton Claudius D III as the reviser's exemplar; current scholarship says the reviser and compiler used different Latin texts.

open_questions.question: Were the English adaptation and the surviving Cotton copy made at Wintney Priory, or does the evidence establish only later use and ownership there?
next_steps: Review palaeographical, codicological, and institutional scholarship on the manuscript's production.
Keep Wintney as an association and provenance until direct production evidence is found.

open_questions.question: Do all 206 historical U-M TIFF page images remain publicly retrievable through current item-specific endpoints?
next_steps: Retry the stable resolver and item interface without automated-client blocking.
Ask U-M Digital Collections for a current IIIF or image-service inventory if needed.
Continue using the independently verified complete BSB edition facsimile for reproducible page inspection.

notes: Schröer's a/b folio milestones preserve a superseded foliation one leaf behind the modern manuscript foliation and begin the English prologue at old folio 52a/current f. 54r; retain this crosswalk instead of silently relabeling the XML milestones.
Printed page 11 and BSB canvas 43 contain the continuation of the Latin chapter list, not target English prose; English chapter I begins on printed page 13/BSB canvas 45.

The complete BSB bundle is a facsimile of the 1888 edition carrier and the complete BL bundle is a facsimile of the manuscript carrier. Complete capture of either does not collapse edition, witness, or intellectual work layers.
